text:
As someone who doesn't know much about Flutter, I'm curious what about it looks appealing to you as a "host environment for visual programming environment experiments".

The article you linked to includes three desiderata that Flutter supposedly fulfills (screenshotted below). But other than the cross-platform stuff – which matters to some, but certainly not all, programming experiments – they seem like criteria Typescript + HTML5 would accommodate. Or not, if there are ways Flutter goes beyond that I don't know about! But I haven't seen a specific articulation of how that might be.

(A natural question this is raising for me is, what WOULD I want out of a host environment? That's a good, separate, question.)

text: <@UBKNXPBAB> Native app on multiple platforms (with wasm in the browser) plus an extremely sophisticated <https://skia.org/user/modules/canvaskit|graphics library>. It's a great reduction of the barrier to making very performant cross-platform GUIs, at least in theory.

text: <@UBKNXPBAB> agreeing with <@U013ZLJARC7> - you can expect it to be faster with relatively similar primitives as react+TS, minus the DOM weirdness. Its far from perfect though - JSX remains more ergonomic than their weird constructor composition, packages kinda suck, and text support is very strange (since it's all virtual)

text: Honestly for me, it's layout and endless CSS pain that draws me into other stacks. GToolkit was another one on my radar for this reason

text: Random opinions: Flutter is super appealing if you don’t want to spend years learning the insanity of browser dev. That’s why I originally started with Dart, but ended up having to bite the bullet. Also if you need silky animation (React Native maybe is OK too, don’t know). If you want to develop a UI programming environment, Flutter has a much simpler model to translate into. But browser dev has like 100x community size, which matters a lot.

text:
I came across this paper/dissertation: _Text in Diagrams: Challenges to and Opportunities of Automatic Layout,_ which perhaps deserves its own post at some point. It's by Christoph Daniel Schulze, who, at least at one point, seemed to be involved in the Eclipse graph data structure I posted.

<https://d-nb.info/120297998X/34>

text:
Nah, I didn't. :disappointed: I think what I really want is something like the above but a bit different and more streamlined, it's just that my data structure and algorithm chops aren't that great.

I am thinking about my own visual languages, and I think constrained automatic layout is essential. However, it's extremely hard, and it seems one could spend their whole career on just that problem alone.

text:
You could have a look at some of the algorithms used by GraphViz. They seem to have a lot of their algorithms documented here: <https://graphviz.org/theory/>
Some look quite adaptable based on what you want to optimize for: e.g. this seems to be a classic approach (where vertices have repulsion forces applied to them) <https://reingold.co/force-directed.pdf>, adapted here with attractive forces for grouping similar items: <https://watermark.silverchair.com/170853.pdf?token=AQECAHi208BE49Ooan9kkhW_Ercy7Dm3ZL_9Cf3qfKAc485ysgAAArswggK3BgkqhkiG9w0BBwagggKoMIICpAIBADCCAp0GCSqGSIb3DQEHATAeBglghkgBZQMEAS4wEQQMXPtAH97l_F-zFar-AgEQgIICbs7tpUGvgimQdG0xZE3p08lLt-BiOxxlZS8cmA0m4M8d51Nbuizrk5Cry1yGdtk_N4RG8BjfP-zT-sMKIKND9drj-brLrEL07UMdxazkC17tlw_W4hHUhEqb0maiHnkqSHdOAkRWVDRifcu4IdvghBYh7Gww8IUe5PZqwCdc8p0pdWpUAsrpNOW7pT5Ikb0dIQniwoMs01YwUrIVLbq7Kg1ikBQRpiZM8YL425dLuQ-ngMsZDs62yNNZgHKIFda8QDMoCUaOUAOvv_llCv65fuJerBBD_ivuB5_dkE2pfSecqpJOIa0LSFpfWjaiCp51kJbG-xqt3d73IovYARK9YE1bvyjArAzG3JRe_P3AKPfR5ImXc0BZUCFBCH-ygMQ5vS7Wc1S4aZDYqaahz1rhweVlmD4HUsFT1Yc_leWL56d7Avv7knGDcBldvHKPZW5RjcmC1JXB-nEwC8c77PxeDaEtr8g7HR7Wa2ziAJEnpYMVaJ8f7aMWomMpR18pZ9wytwZXpzHr68ynU_9d7hRbMk87fnRVU5z8zuM6W57pB2wa_flk8ibhS-G3M6WjTTVO5vJ6MsENYUbAd6gKV5CxGcW5roIeBYuGQVu8fC0EkmOdt5Dr372UNEmVors_w5FWFqRlbLTY9T4Fz_7g6Yjl09eAoDOG-1wwKLfyJptPZl7JnBlu862iyv2dd1YLcvqzQUYNy68wWd5cCRTqhh1gyWbO0DlUKyhsE71wQyuacwuvgTH7Xqn9-xw4QGRj6vfQbbjjo3Yo84mwHYVnJxOba-siUVK56hNDguWSwEx-g7GAC0T1tFQQWxAPSNfgf4U|https://watermark.silverchair.com/170853.pdf?token=AQECAHi208BE49Ooan9kkhW_Ercy7Dm3ZL_9Cf[…]o84mwHYVnJxOba-siUVK56hNDguWSwEx-g7GAC0T1tFQQWxAPSNfgf4U>

elements.text: You could have a look at some of the algorithms used by GraphViz. They seem to have a lot of their algorithms documented here:
Some look quite adaptable based on what you want to optimize for: e.g. this seems to be a classic approach (where vertices have repulsion forces applied to them)
, adapted here with attractive forces for grouping similar items:
https://watermark.silverchair.com/170853.pdf?token=AQECAHi208BE49Ooan9kkhW_Ercy7Dm3ZL_9Cf[…]o84mwHYVnJxOba-siUVK56hNDguWSwEx-g7GAC0T1tFQQWxAPSNfgf4U
if you're unfamiliar, GraphViz have a few different styles of presentation:
dot -
“hierarchical” or layered drawings of directed graphs. This is the default tool to use if edges have directionality.
neato -

“spring model” layouts. This is the default tool to use if the graph is not too large (about 100 nodes) and you don't know anything else about it. Neato attempts to minimize a global energy function, which is equivalent to statistical multi-dimensional scaling.
fdp -
“spring model” layouts similar to those of neato, but does this by reducing forces rather than working with energy.
sfdp -
multiscale version of fdp for the layout of large graphs.
twopi -
radial layouts, after Graham Wills 97. Nodes are placed on concentric circles depending their distance from a given root node.
circo -
circular layout, after Six and Tollis 99, Kauffman and Wiese 02. This is suitable for certain diagrams of multiple cyclic structures, such as certain telecommunications networks.

text: I came across this paper looking at making graph edges less of a rat's nest while still conveying all of the same info <https://aviz.fr/~bbach/confluentgraphs/>

Tasty

text:
Thanks <@UEDDR6L5Q>! I have been aware of GraphViz but was worried that its algorithms are too generic. That paper you linked looks interesting indeed.

I have written a lot of LabVIEW in my career, and in thinking about my own visual language, I would love to have a layout algorithm that basically copies my own internal algorithm that I follow to have beautifully laid out data flow code (minimal wire crossings, “clothesline's, left to right flow, spacing, etc). But there’s a huge gap between the dynamic algorithm I use to getting one on paper, so to speak.

elements.text: Thanks
! I have been aware of GraphViz but was worried that its algorithms are too generic. That paper you linked looks interesting indeed.

I have written a lot of LabVIEW in my career, and in thinking about my own visual language, I would love to have a layout algorithm that basically copies my own internal algorithm that I follow to have beautifully laid out data flow code (minimal wire crossings, “clothesline's, left to right flow, spacing, etc). But there’s a huge gap between the dynamic algorithm I use to getting one on paper, so to speak.
